revision.comment: initial text of stub article -- 1st steps in a constructive direction
revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Akama Shrine
赤間神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | width = | caption = Tthe ''[[haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at Shiramine Jingu (Shiramine Shrine), Kamigyo–ku, Kyoto in honor of the Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known in as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[http://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Akama''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Akama's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Akama ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Viscissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Viciissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} [[ja:白峯神宮]]

[http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. revision.text:
{{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | width = | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity [[Seidai Myojin]] who is popularly known in as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[http://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shriamine's status was changed {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jinjū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Viscissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Viciissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] {{coord missing|Kyoto Prefecture}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto Prefecture]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[ja:白峯神宮]]

This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | width = | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity [[Seidai Myojin]] who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[http://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | width = | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity [[Seidai Myojin]] who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[http://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers.Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals==

'''Shunki Reitaisai Festival'''

(Grand Festival of Spring)

april 14th

Kemari

'''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival'''

July 7

Komachi-odori

Kemari

July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers.Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals==

'''Shunki Reitaisai Festival'''

(Grand Festival of Spring)

april 14

[[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m.

'''Budō(武道）shoureisai'''

(Festival of Budō(武道）)

May 5

Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long)

'''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival'''

July 7

[[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m.

Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m.

==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [http://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [http://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/oclc/186605327 OCLC 186605327] ==External links== [http://www10.ocn.ne.jp/~siramine Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] [http://www10.ocn.ne.jp/~siramine/img216.jpg Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] {{Shinto-stub}} {{Shinto shrine}}

''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [http://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{Infobox Shinto shrine | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | width = | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | type = Imperial Shrine | dedication = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded = | closed = | founder = | priest = | address = | phone = | website = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity [[Seidai Myojin]] who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[http://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers.Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title}} {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]]

revision.comment: /* External links */
revision.text:
{{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity [[Seidai Myojin]] who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers.Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} [[Category:Beppo Shrines]] {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]]

Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. [[Category:Kanpo-taisya]] ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&dq=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan&client=firefox-a&pgis=1 ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&dq=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines&ei=O6yMSZu3HKD2MsWNhdwC&client=firefox-a ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&dq=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno&client=firefox-a ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]]

revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. [[Category:Kanpei-taisha]] ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''Kanpei-chūsha''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]]

[[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]]

revision.comment: Dekimasu moved page [[Shiramine Jingū]] to [[Shiramine Shrine]]: Revert undiscussed move ([[WP:RMUM]]): previous title seems to be appropriate in this case, since the shrine has been known under different names in Japanese, all of which can reasonably be translated to English as "shrine"

In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]][[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Uncategorized_Shinto_shrines]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Improperly tagged templates and modules2]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

(1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia6]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia6]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]][[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia5]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia6]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia6]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia6]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

[http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia38]] [[Category:Articles tagged as templates and modules]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia38]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia35]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia04]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from wikipedia22]] [[Category:dd]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]][[en:Shiramine Jingu]]
