revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} ]] ]] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:11 4]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:bump 1-26]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[sn:Shiramine Jingu]]

[http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:11 4]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[sn:Shiramine Jingu]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:11 4]] [[Category:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:C111]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[sn:Shiramine Jingu]]

This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Shiramine Jingu]][[Category:qq]] }} [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[sn:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:New]]

{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = [[Emperor Junnin]], [[Emperor Sutoku]] | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in [[Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto]]Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[Emperor Junnin]] and [[Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] [[Category:qq]] }} [[sn:Shiramine Jingu]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

[[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Shiramine Jingu]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]]

[[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Pages that used to use Authority control]] [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:beppyo shrines without templates]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 4]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Shiramine Jingu]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis]]

[[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Shinto shrine}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[sn:Shiramine Jingu]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:pages with valid en interwikis 2]]

Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[:en:Kemari|Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[:en:Kemari|Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Kemari|Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of [[:en:Emperor Junnin|Emperor Junnin]] and [[:en:Emperor Sutoku|Emperor Sutoku]]. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 [[:en:Kemari|Kemari]] 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 [[:en:Kemari|Kemari]] 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * [[:en:List of Jingū|List of Jingū]] * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * [[:en:Kemari|Kemari]] ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * [[:en:Richard Ponsonby-Fane|Ponsonby-Fane]], Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Missing wikidata]]

revision.comment: v25: Convert interwiki links [[:en:...]] to {{ill}} templates
revision.text:
{{Short description|Shinto shrine in Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = Shiramine Shrine
白峯神宮 | image = Shiramine-jingu haiden.jpeg | caption = The ''[[haiden (Shinto)|haiden]]'' of Shiramine-jingū, Kamigyō, | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35.0303|N|135.753|E|source:kolossus-jawiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = Imperial Shrine | deity = {{ill|Emperor Junnin|en|Emperor Junnin|WD=Q329713|lt=Emperor Junnin}}, {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Emperor Sutoku}} | founded_by = | established = | date_destroyed = | location = | website = | architecture_style = | festival = | leadership = }} {{nihongo|'''Shiramine Shrine'''|白峯神宮|Shiramine jingū}} is a Shinto Shrine in {{ill|Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|en|Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto|WD=Q1321824|lt=Kamigyō-ku, Kyoto}}Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 126. The Shrine is dedicated to the veneration of the ''kami'' of {{ill|Emperor Junnin|en|Emperor Junnin|WD=Q329713|lt=Emperor Junnin}} and {{ill|Emperor Sutoku|en|Emperor Sutoku|WD=Q349287|lt=Emperor Sutoku}}. Annually in mid–September two Noh performances are held at the Shiramine Shrine in memory of Emperor Sutoku.Kerr, Amy. [http://www.artflock.com/blog/post/toyokuni-jinja-annual-festival-noh-plays-at-shiramine-shrine-toaji-temple-special-opening-markets-japan-post-day/ "Noh Plays at Shiramine Shrine."] September 2008. Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari}} 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari}} 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Jingū|en|List of Jingū|WD=Q120680118|lt=List of Jingū}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] * _______________. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * _______________. (1963). [https://books.google.com/books?id=Uru8GMeblXgC&q=sapporo+shrine+yoshihisa-shinno ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. {{OCLC|186605327}} ==External links== {{Commonscat|Shiramine-jingū}} * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/ Shiramine Shrine Official Site (English, Japanese)] * [http://shiraminejingu.or.jp/omamori/kanauwa/ Photos of (叶う輪 Kanauwa)] {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{draft categories| [[Category:Jingū]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Emperor Sutoku]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Kanpei Chūsha]] }} [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[en:Shiramine Jingu]] [[Category:Jingus without interwikis]]

Shiramine is also home to the deity Seidai Myojin who is popularly known as the god of sports, and especially soccer.[https://www.reuters.com/article/lifestyleMolt/idUSSP30406620070717 "Japan shrine keeps ancient soccer alive and kicking,"] Reuters. July 17, 2007. [[File:Shiramine Shrine.JPG|thumb|Shrine exterior]] The lucky charm(叶う輪 Kanauwa) of Shiramine Shrine is very popular to worshipers. Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports. [[File:叶う輪 主なタイプ.jpg|thumb|Kanauwa is Lucky charm of sports.]] [[File:小町踊り.jpg|thumb|Komachi-odori]] ==Kanpei-sha== In 1871, the {{nihongo|''Kanpei-sha''|官幣社}} identified the hierarchy of government-supported shrines most closely associated with the Imperial family.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' p. 124. The ''kampeisha'' were shrines venerated by the imperial family. This Category encompasses those sanctuaries enshrining emperors, imperial family members, or meritorious retainers of the Imperial family.Institute for Japanese Culture and Classics, Kokugakuin University: [http://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/glossary/def_K.html Glossary of Shinto Names and Terms, ''Kampei Taisha.''] Up through 1940, the mid-range of Imperial shrines or {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-chūsha]]''|官幣中社}} included the shrine; and it was then known as Shiramine''-gū''Ponsonby-Fane. ''Imperial,'' p. 125. In 1940, Shiramine's status was changed to {{nihongo|''Kanpei-taisha''|官幣大社}}, which is the highest Rank; and since then, it has been known as Shiramine ''jingū.''Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1963). ''The Vicissitudes of Shinto,'' p. 394. ==Festivals== '''Shunki Reitaisai Festival''' (Grand Festival of Spring) April 14 {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} 10:30 a.m. '''Budō(武道）shoureisai''' (Festival of Budō(武道）) May 5 Japanese Budō Demonstration from9:00 a.m.(all day long) '''Seidaimyoujin Reisai Festival''' July 7 {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} 2:00 p.m. Komachi-odori 4:30 p.m. ==See also== * {{ill|List of Jingū|en|List of Jingū|WD=Q120680118|lt=List of Jingū|1=List of Jingū}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] * {{ill|Kemari|en|Kemari|WD=Q12224003|lt=Kemari|1=Kemari}} ==Notes== {{reflist|2}} ==References== * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}, Richard Arthur Brabazon.
